findings.description: The gender dimension in research and innovation content is superficially dismissed rather than appropriately considered. The methodology states 'no gender-specific hypotheses apply to agent architecture research; use cases serve diverse end-user populations by design' without analysing gender-relevant aspects that are material to the proposed work — for example, gender bias in clinical training datasets used in the healthcare demonstrator (OBJ-4), or gendered patterns of interaction with AI agent interfaces. The evaluation form sub-criterion explicitly requires 'appropriate consideration of the gender dimension in research and innovation content,' and a one-sentence dismissal does not meet the grade descriptor for a score of 4 or 5.
findings.evidence: "Gender dimension in R&I content: no gender-specific hypotheses apply to agent architecture research; use cases serve diverse end-user populations by design." (B.1.2)
findings.recommendation: Add a substantive paragraph in B.1.2 addressing gender dimension in R&I content: (a) analysis of potential gender bias in clinical decision-support datasets and mitigation through stratified evaluation in WP5; (b) consideration of gendered interaction patterns with AI agents in user studies; (c) explicit statement on how FAIR data management will include sex-disaggregated reporting where relevant.

findings.description: The evaluation form sub-criterion for methodology includes 'engagement of citizens, civil society and end users where appropriate.' The methodology section does not address engagement of citizens or civil society at all. End-user engagement is mentioned only indirectly through clinical validation with human experts in WP5. For the manufacturing (WP6) and logistics (WP7) demonstrators, no end-user engagement strategy is described despite TRL-5 validation requiring user-facing evaluation.
findings.evidence: "SSH: proportionate to the primarily technical research; WP5 includes clinical validation with human experts addressing responsible AI in healthcare." (B.1.2) — no mention of citizen, civil society, or end-user engagement for WP6/WP7.
findings.recommendation: Add to B.1.2 a description of end-user engagement: (a) how manufacturing operators at ISIA and logistics operators at ELI will participate in demonstrator design and evaluation; (b) whether any advisory board or civil society stakeholder consultation is planned; (c) how user feedback will be integrated into iterative design cycles in WP5–WP7.

findings.description: The beyond-state-of-the-art argument is compressed into two sentences asserting that MAESTRO co-designs planning, memory, coordination, and tool orchestration as a unified architecture where current research treats them as disconnected. No specific competing systems, frameworks, or recent publications are cited for comparison, and the quantified gaps that the MAESTRO approach is designed to close are not explained. An evaluator assessing ambition needs more than an assertion of fragmentation in the field.
findings.evidence: "Beyond state of the art: MAESTRO co-designs planning, memory, coordination, and tool orchestration as a unified architecture where current research treats LLM-based agents, classical multi-agent systems, and memory-augmented neural architectures as disconnected threads." (B.1.1)
findings.recommendation: Expand the beyond-state-of-the-art paragraph in B.1.1 to: (a) name 2–3 specific competing frameworks or recent systems (e.g., AutoGPT, LangChain agent frameworks, specific multi-agent coordination baselines) and identify their limitations; (b) explain why the unified architecture approach is expected to yield the quantified improvements claimed in OBJ-1 through OBJ-3.

findings.description: The evaluation form Impact sub-criterion explicitly includes 'communication activities' alongside dissemination and exploitation. Communication is addressed in a single sentence in B.2.2 ('BAL leads public engagement under WP9 T9-03 including project website, social media, and stakeholder events targeting the AI research community, industrial adopters, and policy makers') without specifying concrete communication actions, timelines, target audience segmentation, or mechanisms for reaching non-specialist publics. For a score of 4 or 5, the grade descriptors require this sub-criterion to be well addressed with at most minor shortcomings.
findings.evidence: "Communication: BAL leads public engagement under WP9 T9-03 including project website, social media, and stakeholder events targeting the AI research community, industrial adopters, and policy makers." (B.2.2) — single sentence covering all communication activities.
findings.recommendation: Expand the communication subsection in B.2.2 to include: (a) specific communication actions (e.g., press releases at demonstrator milestones, public-facing videos, policy briefs); (b) timeline for key communication events aligned with project milestones; (c) strategy for reaching non-specialist audiences including the general public; (d) metrics for communication reach and engagement.

findings.description: Several pathway KPI targets are stated without supporting evidence of their achievability, weakening the credibility argument. For example, '≥500 GitHub stars within 6 months of release' and '≥10 European SMEs/startups building on D9-02 by M48' are asserted without reference to comparable open-source AI projects, community-building precedents, or market analysis. The Impact sub-criterion asks evaluators to assess 'credibility of the pathways,' which requires justification for why stated targets are realistic.
findings.evidence: "OBJ-7 (Open framework release and ecosystem contribution) targeting ≥500 GitHub stars within 6 months of release, AI-on-demand platform contributions, ≥2 TEF validations, AND ≥3 EDIH transfers" (B.2.1) — targets stated without benchmarking or justification.
findings.recommendation: In B.2.1 or B.2.2, add brief justification for the most visible KPI targets: (a) cite comparable open-source AI agent framework adoption rates to benchmark the 500-star target; (b) reference existing EDIH engagement or letters of intent to support the SME uptake targets; (c) explain the basis for the ≥10 SME/startup adoption claim.

findings.criterion: Quality and efficiency of the implementation
findings.description: The evaluation form sub-criterion requires evaluators to assess 'appropriateness of the effort assigned to work packages, and the resources overall.' Section B.3.1 contains no person-month figures, no effort breakdown by partner or work package, and no discussion of resource adequacy or allocation rationale. The cross-section consistency check (CC-08) confirms 'no specific budget figures, person-month values, or cost allocations are cited in any of the three sections.' An evaluator reading Part B cannot assess effort appropriateness from this section, which is a mandatory sub-criterion aspect.
findings.evidence: "Resources validated via budget gate (pass, all WPs and partners covered)." (B.3.1) — this is an internal orchestration reference, not evaluator-facing effort justification. CC-08 confirms no PM values appear.
findings.recommendation: Add to B.3.1: (a) a summary table or narrative of total person-months per work package; (b) effort distribution across partners showing proportionality to their roles; (c) brief justification of resource adequacy for the most effort-intensive WPs (WP2, WP5, WP8); (d) explanation of how the overall effort level is commensurate with the project's ambition and 48-month duration.

findings.criterion: Quality and efficiency of the implementation
findings.description: The evaluation form sub-criterion explicitly asks for 'assessment of risks.' Section B.3.1 mentions '11 risks (technical, external, organisational) with assigned mitigation' but does not describe any specific risk, its likelihood, impact, or mitigation strategy. An evaluator cannot assess the quality of risk management from a count alone. The risk register is referenced but its content is not presented in the evaluator-facing text.
findings.evidence: "Risk register: 11 risks (technical, external, organisational) with assigned mitigation." (B.3.1) — risks referenced by count only, no substantive discussion.
findings.recommendation: Add to B.3.1 a risk assessment subsection presenting at least the 3–4 highest-impact risks with: (a) risk description; (b) likelihood and impact rating; (c) specific mitigation measures; (d) contingency actions if the risk materialises. Focus on risks most likely to concern evaluators: technical integration risk across the three pillars, clinical data access constraints, and open-source community sustainability.

findings.criterion: Quality and efficiency of the implementation
findings.description: The evaluation form sub-criterion asks evaluators to assess 'capacity and role of each participant, and the extent to which the consortium as a whole brings together the necessary expertise.' Section B.3.2 lists each partner's expertise area and WP leadership but provides no evidence of track record — no prior project references, no relevant publications, no previous EU project participation, and no demonstrated achievements. An evaluator assessing capacity expects evidence, not just stated expertise.
findings.evidence: "Alpenstadt Technical University (ATU, DE, coordinator) leads WP1 and WP2, providing expertise in neuro-symbolic AI, automated planning, and LLM-based reasoning." (B.3.2) — expertise is claimed without supporting evidence of track record.
findings.recommendation: Add to B.3.2 for each partner: (a) 1–2 key relevant publications or prior projects demonstrating the claimed expertise; (b) reference to relevant infrastructure or facilities; (c) for the coordinator, evidence of prior coordination experience in EU-funded projects. This is standard practice for competitive HE RIA proposals.

revision_actions.action_description: Insert an effort allocation summary in B.3.1 presenting person-months per work package and per partner, with a narrative justifying the proportionality of effort to task complexity and partner roles. Source the figures from the validated budget response in the integration layer.

Replace the single-sentence gender dimension dismissal in B.1.2 with a substantive paragraph analysing gender-relevant aspects: bias risks in clinical datasets for OBJ-4, gendered user interaction patterns, and sex-disaggregated data reporting in the DMP.

Add end-user and civil society engagement description in B.1.2 covering: user participation in WP5–WP7 demonstrator design and evaluation, any advisory board or stakeholder consultation mechanism, and how user feedback feeds iterative development.
Expand the communication subsection in B.2.2 with specific communication actions, a timeline linked to milestones, a strategy for non-specialist audiences, and measurable engagement targets.
Add a risk assessment subsection in B.3.1 presenting the 3–4 highest-impact risks with descriptions, likelihood/impact ratings, mitigation measures, and contingency plans.
Add track record evidence for each partner in B.3.2: 1–2 relevant prior projects or publications, infrastructure references, and coordinator's EU project management experience.
Expand the beyond-state-of-the-art paragraph in B.1.1 to name 2–3 competing frameworks, identify their specific limitations, and explain why the unified architecture approach addresses those gaps.
Add brief credibility justification for key open-source and SME adoption KPIs in B.2.1 or B.2.2, citing comparable project adoption rates or supporting evidence such as letters of intent.
